title: The Precise Definition of a Limit

epigraph.text: A false balance is an abomination to the Lord, but a just weight is his delight.
epigraph.quote: God made the integers; all else is the work of man.

content.text:
Throughout this chapter, we have relied on an intuitive understanding of
limits: "$f(x)$ gets arbitrarily close to $L$ as $x$ approaches $a$." This
language has guided us well. We have computed limits, proved algebraic rules,
and applied the Squeeze Theorem. But a careful reader should feel a growing
unease. What, precisely, does "arbitrarily close" mean? How close is
"close enough"? And how can we *prove* that a limit equals a particular
value, rather than merely observe it from a table or a graph?

Mathematics demands precision. If we are to build the entire edifice of
calculus on the concept of a limit, we need a definition that is logically
airtight — one that converts our intuition into a statement that can be
verified or refuted without ambiguity. This is the $\varepsilon$-$\delta$
definition, and it is one of the great intellectual achievements of
nineteenth-century mathematics.
Before we state the formal definition, let us see the idea in action.

Suppose we claim that $\limto{x \to 2}(3x - 1) = 5$. A skeptic challenges us:
"Prove it. I want $f(x)$ to be within $0.1$ of $5$." In mathematical notation,
the skeptic demands $\abs{(3x - 1) - 5} < 0.1$, i.e., $\abs{3x - 6} < 0.1$,
i.e., $3\abs{x - 2} < 0.1$, i.e., $\abs{x - 2} < 0.0\overline{3}$.

So we respond: "Take any $x$ within $0.03$ of $2$ (but not equal to $2$), and
$f(x)$ will be within $0.1$ of $5$." The skeptic is satisfied — for now.

But the skeptic can tighten the challenge: "Now I want $f(x)$ within $0.001$
of $5$." Fine: we need $\abs{x - 2} < 0.001/3 \approx 0.000333$. Again, we
find a suitable range.

The critical insight is this: *no matter how small the skeptic's tolerance*,
we can always find a range of $x$-values near $a$ that keeps $f(x)$ within
that tolerance of $L$. The skeptic names the precision; we produce the proximity.

content.title: The Precise Definition of a Limit
content.content:
Let $f$ be a function defined on some open interval containing $a$, except
possibly at $a$ itself. We say
\[
 \limto{x \to a} f(x) = L
\]
if for every number $\varepsilon > 0$ there exists a number $\delta > 0$ such that
\[
 0 < \abs{x - a} < \delta \implies \abs{f(x) - L} < \varepsilon.
\]

content.text:
*Unpacking the definition.**
This single sentence encodes a precise logical structure. Let us examine each piece.

items:
**"For every $\varepsilon > 0$"** — The number $\varepsilon$
 (epsilon) represents the skeptic's challenge: a tolerance around $L$. The
 word "every" is crucial. It is not enough to find $\delta$ for one particular
 $\varepsilon$; we must succeed for *all* positive $\varepsilon$, no matter
 how small.

**"There exists a $\delta > 0$"** — The number $\delta$ (delta)
 is our response: a proximity around $a$. The $\delta$ we choose may (and
 usually does) depend on $\varepsilon$. Smaller challenges demand tighter
 responses.

**"$0 < \abs{x - a} < \delta$"** — This says $x$ is within
 $\delta$ of $a$, but $x \neq a$. We never evaluate $f$ at $a$ itself; the
 limit describes behavior *near* $a$.

**"$\implies \abs{f(x) - L} < \varepsilon$"** — This says $f(x)$
 is within $\varepsilon$ of $L$. The function value is captured in the
 tolerance band.

content.text:
*The geometric picture.**
Draw the horizontal strip $L - \varepsilon < y < L + \varepsilon$ around the
target value. The definition asserts that we can always find a vertical strip
$a - \delta < x < a + \delta$ (with $x \neq a$) such that the graph of $f$ lies
entirely within the horizontal strip whenever $x$ is in the vertical strip. The
challenge: for *any* horizontal strip, no matter how narrow, we can find a
vertical strip that works.

content.title: The Logic Matters
content.content:
The quantifier order in the definition is essential:
\[
 \textbf{For every } \varepsilon > 0, \quad \textbf{there exists } \delta > 0
 \quad\text{such that}\ldots
\]
Reversing the order — "there exists $\delta > 0$ such that for every
$\varepsilon > 0\ldots$" — would mean a single $\delta$ works for all
tolerances, which is almost never true. The $\delta$ is allowed to depend on
$\varepsilon$; indeed, it usually must.

content.title: The Two-Phase Approach
content.content:
1. **Scratch work (find $\delta$).** Starting from the goal
 $\abs{f(x) - L} < \varepsilon$, work backwards to discover what restriction
 on $\abs{x - a}$ is needed. This phase is informal — it is where you
 figure out the right $\delta$.

2. **Formal proof (verify $\delta$ works).** State: "Let
 $\varepsilon > 0$ be given. Choose $\delta = \ldots$\," Then show that
 $0 < \abs{x - a} < \delta$ implies $\abs{f(x) - L} < \varepsilon$. This
 phase is the polished argument.
The scratch work is for you; the formal proof is for the reader. In your
written work, present only the formal proof (but we will show both phases
in our examples for pedagogical clarity).

content.title: A linear limit
content.solution:
**Scratch work.** We need $\abs{(4x - 5) - 7} < \varepsilon$, i.e.,
$\abs{4x - 12} < \varepsilon$, i.e., $4\abs{x - 3} < \varepsilon$, i.e.,
$\abs{x - 3} < \varepsilon/4$. This suggests $\delta = \varepsilon/4$.

**Proof.** Let $\varepsilon > 0$ be given. Choose $\delta = \varepsilon/4$.
Then whenever $0 < \abs{x - 3} < \delta$, we have
\[
 \abs{(4x - 5) - 7} = \abs{4x - 12} = 4\abs{x - 3} < 4\delta = 4 \cdot \frac{\varepsilon}{4} = \varepsilon.
\]
Therefore $\limto{x \to 3}(4x - 5) = 7$.

content.title: Another linear limit
content.solution:
**Scratch work.** We need $\abs{(2 - 3x) - 5} < \varepsilon$, i.e.,
$\abs{-3x - 3} < \varepsilon$, i.e., $3\abs{x + 1} < \varepsilon$, i.e.,
$\abs{x - (-1)} < \varepsilon/3$. So $\delta = \varepsilon/3$.

**Proof.** Let $\varepsilon > 0$ be given. Choose $\delta = \varepsilon/3$.
Then whenever $0 < \abs{x - (-1)} < \delta$:
\[
 \abs{(2 - 3x) - 5} = \abs{-3x - 3} = 3\abs{x + 1} < 3\delta
 = 3 \cdot \frac{\varepsilon}{3} = \varepsilon.
\]
Therefore $\limto{x \to -1}(2 - 3x) = 5$.

content.title: A quadratic limit --- the standard trick
content.solution:
**Scratch work.** We need $\abs{x^2 - 4} < \varepsilon$. Factor:
$\abs{x^2 - 4} = \abs{x-2}\abs{x+2}$. The factor $\abs{x - 2}$ is what we
control with $\delta$. But $\abs{x + 2}$ also depends on $x$ — we need to
bound it.

If we assume $\delta \leq 1$, then $\abs{x - 2} < 1$ implies $1 < x < 3$,
so $3 < x + 2 < 5$, and thus $\abs{x + 2} < 5$. Under this assumption:
\[
 \abs{x^2 - 4} = \abs{x-2}\abs{x+2} < 5\abs{x-2}.
\]
We want $5\abs{x-2} < \varepsilon$, so $\abs{x-2} < \varepsilon/5$.
Taking $\delta = \min(1,\, \varepsilon/5)$ ensures both conditions hold.

**Proof.** Let $\varepsilon > 0$ be given. Choose
$\delta = \min(1,\, \varepsilon/5)$. Suppose $0 < \abs{x-2} < \delta$.

Since $\delta \leq 1$, we have $\abs{x-2} < 1$, so $1 < x < 3$, hence
$\abs{x+2} < 5$.

Since $\delta \leq \varepsilon/5$, we have $\abs{x-2} < \varepsilon/5$.

Therefore:
\[
 \abs{x^2 - 4} = \abs{x-2}\abs{x+2} < \frac{\varepsilon}{5} \cdot 5 = \varepsilon.
\]
This proves $\limto{x \to 2} x^2 = 4$.

content.title: The Role of $\min$
content.content:
In the previous proof, we chose $\delta = \min(1, \varepsilon/5)$. The
"$\min$" is not mysterious: it simply ensures that $\delta$ satisfies
*both* requirements simultaneously. The restriction $\delta \leq 1$
lets us bound $\abs{x+2}$; the restriction $\delta \leq \varepsilon/5$
gives us the final inequality. Any smaller $\delta$ would also work —
$\delta$ only needs to be *small enough*, not optimal.

content.text: Precise Definitions of One-Sided and Infinite Limits
The $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ framework extends naturally to other types of limits.

content.title: Precise Definition: One-Sided Limits
content.content:
We say $\limto{x \to a^+} f(x) = L$ if for every $\varepsilon > 0$, there exists
$\delta > 0$ such that
\[
 0 < x - a < \delta \implies \abs{f(x) - L} < \varepsilon.
\]
Similarly, $\limto{x \to a^-} f(x) = L$ if for every $\varepsilon > 0$, there
exists $\delta > 0$ such that
\[
 0 < a - x < \delta \implies \abs{f(x) - L} < \varepsilon.
\]

content.title: Precise Definition: Infinite Limits
content.content:
We say $\limto{x \to a} f(x) = \infty$ if for every number $M > 0$, there exists
$\delta > 0$ such that
\[
 0 < \abs{x - a} < \delta \implies f(x) > M.
\]

content.title: Proving a limit is infinite
content.solution:
Let $M > 0$ be given. We need $\frac{1}{x^2} > M$, which holds when
$x^2 < \frac{1}{M}$, i.e., $\abs{x} < \frac{1}{\sqrt{M}}$.

Choose $\delta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{M}}$. Then whenever $0 < \abs{x - 0} < \delta$:
\[
 \abs{x} < \frac{1}{\sqrt{M}} \implies x^2 < \frac{1}{M}
 \implies \frac{1}{x^2} > M.
\]
Therefore $\limto{x \to 0} \frac{1}{x^2} = \infty$.

content.title: Uniqueness of Limits
content.content:
If $\limto{x \to a} f(x) = L_1$ and $\limto{x \to a} f(x) = L_2$, then
$L_1 = L_2$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Suppose for contradiction that $L_1 \neq L_2$. Let $\varepsilon = \abs{L_1 - L_2}/2 > 0$.

Since $\limto{x \to a} f(x) = L_1$, there exists $\delta_1 > 0$ such that
\[
 0 < \abs{x - a} < \delta_1 \implies \abs{f(x) - L_1} < \varepsilon.
\]
Since $\limto{x \to a} f(x) = L_2$, there exists $\delta_2 > 0$ such that
\[
 0 < \abs{x - a} < \delta_2 \implies \abs{f(x) - L_2} < \varepsilon.
\]
Let $\delta = \min(\delta_1, \delta_2)$ and choose any $x$ with
$0 < \abs{x - a} < \delta$. Then by the Triangle Inequality:
\[
 \abs{L_1 - L_2} = \abs{(L_1 - f(x)) + (f(x) - L_2)}
 \leq \abs{f(x) - L_1} + \abs{f(x) - L_2}
 < \varepsilon + \varepsilon = \abs{L_1 - L_2}.
\]
This gives $\abs{L_1 - L_2} < \abs{L_1 - L_2}$, a contradiction. Therefore
$L_1 = L_2$.

content.title: Karl Weierstrass (1815--1897) and Augustin-Louis Cauchy (1789--1857)
content.content:
The precise $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ definition of a limit is often attributed to
Karl Weierstrass, who formalized it in his Berlin lectures during the 1860s.
Weierstrass, sometimes called the "father of modern analysis," insisted on
absolute logical rigor. Before him, mathematicians like Newton, Leibniz, and
even Euler used limits and infinitesimals with brilliant intuition but
sometimes shaky foundations.

Augustin-Louis Cauchy, a devout Catholic, came remarkably close to the modern
definition decades earlier. In his *Cours d'analyse* (1821), Cauchy
wrote that a variable approaches a limit when its values "differ from it by as
little as one wishes" — language strikingly close to
$\varepsilon$-$\delta$, but lacking the precise quantifier structure.

Weierstrass supplied exactly this: the nested "for every… there
exists…" that distinguishes a rigorous proof from a persuasive
argument. It was a victory of precision over intuition — and a reminder
that even the most obvious truths deserve careful demonstration.

marginNotes.text:
All your words are true; all your righteous
laws are eternal.

Every good and perfect gift is from above,
coming down from the Father of the heavenly lights, who does not change
like shifting shadows.

marginNotes.author: Karl Weierstrass
marginNotes.text:
A mathematician who is not also something of
a poet will never be a complete mathematician.
